summary: Gruber amplifies Adrienne LaFrance's Atlantic essay arguing Facebook is a 'Doomsday Machine,' but pushes back on one key claim: Zuckerberg can control Facebook, he simply won't if it reduces engagement. Gruber draws a parallel to tobacco companies, arguing Facebook's toxicity and addictiveness are directly correlated. The core argument centers on Facebook's algorithmic feeds creating 3 billion unique, unknowable versions of the platform, making it fundamentally different from — and worse than — even notorious sites like 8kun. Gruber contends that Facebook's post-election moderation decisions were driven by engagement optimization, not democratic principles. He closes by noting the disturbing reality that one person ultimately controls a platform of this scale.
key_points: Zuckerberg can control Facebook's toxicity but won't because reducing it would reduce the addictiveness that drives engagement — analogous to tobacco companies and cancer

Facebook's algorithmic personalization means 3 billion users see 3 billion different Facebooks, making the platform fundamentally unknowable as a whole — unlike even 8kun, which is at least a visible, printable product
The real regulatory challenge is that Facebook's most consequential 'speech' isn't user posts but the algorithm determining who sees what
Facebook's election-related moderation was likely driven by engagement calculus rather than democratic principles
The concentration of power over this unknowable megascale platform in one person's hands is deeply troubling
money_quotes: What keeps too many people hooked to Facebook is exactly the sort of worldview-warping toxic content that is damaging society worldwide.
I honestly believe Zuckerberg would prefer to reduce the toxicity of Facebook's social media platforms, but not enough to do so if it reduces Facebook's addictiveness.
When you post something to Facebook, the 'speech' that matters most isn't the content of the post but the algorithm that determines who sees it and how.

A mere 'website' — say, Wikipedia — that reaches an audience of billions is like the surface of an ocean: enormously expansive, but visible. Facebook is like the volume of an ocean: not merely massive, but unknowable.

We instinctively think that 8kun is 'worse' than Facebook because its content is worse — what is permitted, and what actually is posted. But Facebook is in fact far worse, because by its nature we, as a whole, can't even see what 'Facebook' is because everyone's feed is unique.
How could you ever say what Facebook is at any given moment, let alone for a given day, let alone as an omnipresent daily presence in billions of people's lives?

Is there any reason to believe that Facebook chose the pre- and post-election course it did because it was the right thing to do — rather than the result of a cold calculus that determined it was the optimal way to keep the most people the most engaged with Facebook?
I know that whatever the reasons, they were ultimately determined by one person. That's quite a thing.
key_insight: Facebook's true danger lies not in what any individual sees but in the unknowable totality of 3 billion algorithmically personalized realities controlled by one person optimizing for engagement above all else.
